# A Map of AI-for-Architecture Work {#sec-appendix-d-ai-for-architecture-map}

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This appendix consolidates the prior body of AI-for-architecture work that Architecture 2.0 builds on. It groups established results into families, maps each family to the architecture capabilities it most advances and to the chapter that develops it, and states how the discipline in this book differs from any single family. The map is a reading aid and a positioning statement, not a survey.}
```

Machine learning has appeared in computer architecture for decades, first inside shipped mechanisms and later as tool-using systems that participate in the design process itself. The families below are the strands this material draws on. Collecting them serves a narrow purpose. It is not another survey, which existing work already provides [@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture], but a positioning aid that shows what each family contributes, which of the six architecture capabilities it most advances, and where this book puts it to use. @tbl-ai-arch-map gives that mapping.

| **Family of work** | **Representative results** | **Capabilities advanced** | **Developed in** | **Key sources** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Learned in-chip components** | Perceptron branch predictors and learned prefetchers, where learning is embedded in the shipped artifact and does a bounded tactical job | Implement, Evaluate | @sec-moonshot, @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization | [@JimenezLin2001Perceptron; @ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture] |
| **Learned cost models and predictive DSE** | Surrogates and regression models that predict performance and power across a design space from cheap features | Explore, Evaluate | @sec-data-representations-world-models, @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization | [@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE; @LeeBrooks2006RegressionModeling] |
| **Autotuning and autoschedulers** | Statistical search over compiler and tensor-program schedules guided by measured hardware | Explore | @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization | [@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM; @ZhengEtAl2020Ansor; @RaganKelleyEtAl2017HalideCACM] |
| **RL for physical design and datapath** | Reinforcement learning for macro placement and datapath synthesis, and commercial design-space tools | Explore, Implement | @sec-architecture-20-ontology, @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack, @sec-what-architect-owns | [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023MacroPlacement; @RoyEtAl2021PrefixRL; @Synopsys2023DSOai] |
| **LLMs for RTL and HLS generation** | Natural-language and high-level input lowered toward RTL, extending the high-level-synthesis lineage | Formulate, Implement | @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces, @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization | [@LiuEtAl2023ChipNeMo; @ThakurEtAl2023VeriGen; @ThakurEtAl2023AutoChip; @CoussyMorawiec2008HighLevelSynthesis] |
| **Agentic, tool-using exploration** | Agents that propose candidates, call tools, read the returned results, and revise a search | Formulate through Defend | @sec-architecture-20-ontology, @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces, @sec-running-the-loop | [@WangEtAl2026AgentDSE; @KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym] |
| **Benchmarks and datasets** | Datasets and question sets that test architectural understanding and reasoning | Evaluate, Explain | @sec-data-representations-world-models, @sec-evaluating-agentic-architect | [@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch] |

: **A map of AI-for-architecture work and where this book develops it.** Each family names prior results, the architecture capabilities it most advances, and the chapters that use it. The unit of work in Architecture 2.0 is the bounded study and its claim, not any single family's artifact. {#tbl-ai-arch-map tbl-colwidths="[20,28,15,22,15]"}

These families are not exclusive. An agentic design-space search may call a learned cost model, invoke an autoscheduler, and emit RTL, meaning a single study can touch several rows at once.

Because these methods often combine, what separates Architecture 2.0 from any single family is its unit of work. A learned branch predictor improves one mechanism. A macro-placement agent improves one implementation step. An RTL-generating model produces one artifact. Each is valuable, and each is judged on its own narrow output.

This material instead takes the bounded study and its reviewable claim as the unit. The question is not just whether a model produced a plausible artifact, but whether the whole loop—from intent through tools to evidence—supports an architectural decision that another architect can inspect and challenge. The families mapped here supply the methods; the discipline supplies the standard of judgment that decides what their outputs are worth.

## Candidates, Not Decisions

Those outputs are always candidates, not decisions. A learned model, an autoscheduler, or an agentic search proposes a macro placement or an RTL block, but that candidate still has to clear the physical-design flow against power, area, timing closure, and routing congestion before it counts. The methods explore the space; the architect signs off on physical correctness. This division of labor is the constant the rest of the book returns to.
